summary: Eight Davidic psalms span the full range of faith — Psalm 6's bone-deep lament for healing, Psalm 8's wonder at human dignity and infant praise silencing enemies, Psalm 9's joy at nations snared in their own pits, Psalm 10's sustained portrait of the wicked who boast they will never be moved, Psalm 14's sweeping diagnosis that none does good, Psalm 16's radical contentment with the LORD as sole portion and inheritance, Psalm 19's twin testimony of creation and Torah, and Psalm 21's royal thanksgiving for a king granted life forever and ever.
psalm_rows_html:
PsalmTypeAuthorSituationKey ThemeNotable VerseNT Connection
6Penitential Lament (Individual)David (superscription)Severe physical illness combined with anguish over enemies; the psalmist pleads for healing and deliverance from those who gloat over his sufferingCrying out from bone-deep distress; God's steadfast love (hesed) as the sole ground of appeal; unexpected shift from despair to confidence that God has heardv.9 — "The LORD has heard the sound of my weeping."One of the seven traditional penitential psalms (with 32, 38, 51, 102, 130, 143); Jesus' soul-anguish in Gethsemane ("My soul is very sorrowful", Matt 26:38) echoes the depths of Ps 6; the sudden turn to assurance models the faith that receives forgiveness
8Hymn of Praise / Creation PsalmDavid (superscription)A meditation on the breathtaking contrast between God's transcendent majesty and his care for fragile, mortal humanity; likely sung in temple worshipHuman dignity as image-bearers crowned with glory; the paradox that the majestic God ordains infant voices to silence cosmic enemies; creation as testimony to gracev.4 — "What is man that you are mindful of him, and the son of man that you care for him?"Heb 2:6–8 quotes Ps 8 and applies it to Jesus as the true Son of Man who fulfills humanity's destiny; Matt 21:16 quotes v.2 when Jesus defends the children's hosannas; 1 Cor 15:27 applies "all things under his feet" to the risen Christ
9Individual Praise / Acrostic (Psalms 9–10 form one poem)David (superscription)Thanksgiving for deliverance from hostile nations; the psalmist celebrates God's just judgment from his heavenly throne and looks to the future vindication of the oppressedGod as righteous judge of the nations; the wicked destroyed by their own devices; the needy not forgotten; divine justice as the only secure ground for the vulnerablev.15 — "The nations have sunk in the pit that they made; in the net that they hid, their own foot has been caught."The retributive justice of Ps 9 reaches its supreme expression in the cross, where the powers that crucified Christ were publicly defeated by the very instrument of their victory (Col 2:15); Rev 11:15 echoes the enthronement theme of v.7
10Individual Lament / Acrostic (continuation of Ps 9)Anonymous (LXX joins with Ps 9; likely Davidic)The psalmist cries out over God's apparent hiddenness while the arrogant wicked oppress the poor and defenseless without consequenceThe anatomy of godless arrogance: denial of divine accountability, exploitation of the weak, and boastful self-sufficiency; God's answering promise to be king and defender of the oppressedv.6 — "He says in his heart, 'I shall not be moved; throughout all generations I shall not meet adversity.'"The oppression of the poor in Ps 10 anticipates James 5:1–6; God's hearing of the humble's cry (v.17) is the basis for Jesus' beatitudes (Matt 5:3–5); the king who "rules forever and ever" (v.16) is fulfilled in Christ's eternal reign
14Wisdom / Communal Lament (nearly identical to Ps 53)David (superscription)God surveys humanity from heaven and finds universal moral corruption; the psalm cries out for salvation to come from Zion to restore the fortunes of his peopleUniversal depravity apart from God; practical atheism ("no God") as the root of all ethical corruption; the certain hope of coming salvationv.1 — "The fool says in his heart, 'There is no God.' They are corrupt, they do abominable deeds; there is none who does good."Paul quotes Ps 14:1–3 at length in Rom 3:10–12 as part of his case that all humanity stands condemned and in need of the righteousness of God through faith in Christ; the "salvation out of Zion" in v.7 anticipates Rom 11:26
16Song of Trust (Miktam)David (superscription)The psalmist confesses radical contentment in the LORD as his sole good and portion, renounces all idolatry, and expresses confidence that even death will not sever the bond between him and GodThe LORD as the psalmist's entire inheritance and cup; the pleasantness of the divine portion; confident trust that God will not abandon his servant to the gravev.11 — "You make known to me the path of life; in your presence there is fullness of joy; at your right hand are pleasures forevermore."Peter quotes Ps 16:8–11 at Pentecost (Acts 2:25–31) as a prophecy of Christ's resurrection, arguing that David could not have meant himself (he died and was buried) but the Holy One who would not see corruption; Paul repeats this argument in Acts 13:35
19Hymn (two-part: Creation + Torah)David (superscription)A meditation on the two great revelations: the heavens declaring God's glory without words, and the Torah declaring his will with words; closing with a prayer for integrity before a holy GodCreation as wordless universal testimony to divine glory; the Torah as perfect, reviving, wise, and more desirable than gold; the need for cleansing from both hidden faults and willful sinsv.14 — "Let the words of my mouth and the meditation of my heart be acceptable in your sight, O LORD, my rock and my redeemer."Paul quotes v.4 ("their voice has gone out to all the earth") in Rom 10:18 to argue that Israel has already heard the gospel; the Torah's role in Ps 19 anticipates the Lutheran distinction: the law reveals sin, while grace (the Redeemer of v.14) provides the remedy
21Royal Psalm (Thanksgiving for Victory)David (superscription)Thanksgiving after the king's military victory; the congregation celebrates all that the LORD has given the king: crown, life, glory, and the defeat of all enemiesThe king's strength rests entirely in the LORD, not in military might; the gift of "life forever and ever" points beyond any historical king to a messianic fulfillment; God's enemies will be consumed like a furnacev.4 — "He asked life of you; you gave it to him, length of days forever and ever."No Davidic king lived "forever and ever," so the church has consistently read Ps 21 as messianic; Peter's Pentecost argument (Acts 2:29–32) applies the same logic to the resurrection: only Christ fully receives the eternal life the king asked for; Heb 7:24–25 identifies Jesus as the priest-king who "continues forever"
